quotes.verbatim_quote:
3. Remember the chord by where the root is; this way assumes that: a) there is a root in the chord somewhere,
b)you are able to locate it; and c) you know the names of the notes on the fingerboard. For instance, suppose you
have never learned this chord:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the third technique for memorizing chord forms — anchoring on the root — and states its three prerequisites explicitly.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The root in this chord is on the 4th string, so if you know the names
of your notes on the 4th string, you will be able to transpose it and memorize it fairly

7 easily by focusing on the root as a visual anchor point, This technique takes practice

U but it works very well, especially as a last resort when the other techniques don't seem

to be doing the job.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains how the root-anchor technique works in practice and establishes when to deploy it as a rule.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
To learn this, you might wish to make up drills like: ‘‘Find the A notes on each string;
likewise with B, C, D, etc.; then Ap, Bd, etc., then A#, B#, etc.” or any other drill you can think up that seems
effective; or you can work on your note reading - this too will eventually teach you the note names on the finger-
board
quotes.why_load_bearing: This provides concrete drill examples for learning note names across all strings, a prerequisite skill for the root-anchor memorization technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most players use a combination of all three methods outlined here, so experiment with all of them and you will
find what works best for you.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the reader a practical rule-of-thumb: eclecticism across all three techniques is the norm among experienced players.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Webster’s dictionary defines the word homonym as “a word with the same pronunciation as another but with a
different meaning.” In other words, the two words sound the same but come from different places (like read and reed
ot principal and principle). In playing through this book, you are going to encounter chord homonyms, that is, chords
that sound the same but have different origins and names.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This formally defines the central concept of the chapter by analogy to linguistic homonyms, establishing the terminological framework for the rest of the discussion.

quotes.verbatim_quote: So, as you can see, the naming of homonyms will be determined by “what is easiest” or Just plain common sense.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This states the governing rule for resolving homonym naming ambiguity: harmonic context and common sense dictate which name applies.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If you are working with the progression, say, Em Am, and you see this chord being used in place of Em, guess
what it’s going to be called (how about Em6?),
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the first of three concrete examples showing how harmonic context determines which name a homonym chord receives.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Likewise, if you are working with C#m7 F#7 Bm and you run into this chord being used in place of C#m7, you
can expect it to be named C#m7b5.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This second homonym example demonstrates that the same chord form takes a minor-seventh-flat-five name when functioning in a minor-key context.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Finally if you are working with Em7 A7 D and you find this chord being used in place of A7, then it will be called
AQ.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This third example completes the set, showing the same chord form named as a dominant ninth when substituting for a V7 chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You will
have to know all the useful names for a chord form or you will just be making it hard for yourself in the long run.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a direct rule requiring the student to learn every valid name for each chord form as a practical necessity.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
These chords are said to be DIATONIC to the key of C (in this book, diatonic means: using only
notes of the scale)
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the book's working definition of 'diatonic,' a foundational term used throughout the chord progressions material.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Analyze, as used here, means to find out what tones of the chord are present and accounted for (many chord
forms leave out Sths or roots or even other tones sometimes).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the analytical method the student must apply to chord diagrams throughout the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
CHORDS BY 3rd INTERVALS

+ Triads — 13 $5 liths —135
; Ith —1357 13ths —~ 135
} Oths ~ 13579
quotes.why_load_bearing: This table encodes the fundamental rule that chords are constructed by stacking thirds and lists expected tones for each chord type, serving as a reference for chord analysis.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
VOICE LEADING (voice leading is the art of connecting each note of a given chord with each note in a following
f chord. See CHORD CHEMISTRY ~ Section 13.)
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines voice leading as the principle behind why chords with missing tones are chosen, linking chord construction choices to an advanced harmonic concept.
